content:
A Fourier transform pair is given by $${\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right)^n}$$ u $$\left[ {n + 3} \right]\,\mathop \Leftrightarrow \limits^{FT} \,{{A{e^{ - j6\pi f}}} \over {1 - \left( {{2 \over 3}} \right){e^{ - j2\pi f}}}}$$ ,
where u(n) donotes the unit step sequence. The values of A is_______________.
Let x(n) = $${\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^n}$$ u(n), y(n) = $${x^2}$$, and Y ($$({e^{j\omega }})\,$$ be the Fourier transform of y(n). Then Y ($$({e^{jo}})$$ is
The radian frequency value(s) for which the discrete time sinusoidal signal $x[n] = A \cos(\Omega n + \pi/3)$ has a period of 40 is/are __.
explanation:
Given :

(i) Discrete time sinusoidal signal

$$ x[n]=A \cos \left(\Omega n+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) $$

(ii) $\quad$ Time period $=40$

Concept :

$$ x(n)=x(n \pm k N) $$

Where, $K=$ Least Integer

$$ \begin{aligned} & N=F T P=\text { Integer } \\ & x(n)=x_1(n) \pm x_2(n) \end{aligned} $$

$N_1$ is the FTP of $x_1(n)$ and $N_2$ is the FTP of $x_2(n)$.

For periodicity of signal $x(n)$.

$$ \frac{N_1}{N_2}=\text { Rational Number. } $$

Time period of ( $n$ ) :

$$ N=\operatorname{LCM}\left[N_1, N_2\right] $$

The sum of two (or) more than two periodic signal. In case of discrete time system will be always periodic.

Complex exponential and sinusoidal signal are always periodic In case of continuous time system.

Complex exponential and sinusoidal signal in case of discrete time system will be periodic only if ratio $\frac{2 \pi}{\omega_0}$ is a Rational Number.

$$ N=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega_0} K $$

$K=$ Least Integer for which ' $N$ ' is an Integer According to question :

$$ x(n)=A \cos \left(\Omega n+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) $$

Note : The period of the periodic signal does not change even if it is time shifted.

From option (A) :

$$ \frac{2 \pi}{\Omega} k=40\,\,\,\,\,\,\, [Given]$$

where: $\left[\Omega=\omega_0\right]$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{2 \pi}{\omega_0} k=40 \\ & \frac{2 \pi}{0.15 \pi} k=\frac{200}{15} k=\frac{40}{3} k=40 \\ & {[k=\text { Least Integer }=3]} \end{aligned} $$

So, option (A) is correct.

From option (B) :

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{2 \pi}{\omega_0} k=40 \\ & \frac{2 \pi k}{0.225 \pi}=\frac{2000}{225} k=\frac{80}{9} k=40 \\ & {[k=\text { least Integer }=9]} \end{aligned} $$

But this option is not satisfied.

So, option (B) is incorrect.

From option (C) :

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \frac{2 \pi}{0.3 \pi} k=40 & \\ \frac{20}{3} k=40 & {[k=3]} \\ \frac{40}{6} k=40 & {[k=6]} \end{array} $$

In option (C) the value of $k$ we cannot take 6 . Because the value of $k$ must be least. Integer for which ' $N$ ' is an Integer. So, in this case least integer value will be 3 and. According to given data this option will not satisfied.

From option (D) :

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{2 \pi}{0.45 \pi} k=40 \\ & \frac{200}{45} k=\frac{40}{9} k=40 \\ & {[k=\text { Least Integer }=9]} \end{aligned} $$

So, option (D) is correct.

Hence, the correct options are (A), (D) OR (A), (C), (D).

content: Consider a discrete-time periodic signal with period N = 5. Let the discrete-time Fourier series (DTFS) representation be $$x[n] = \sum\limits_{k = 0}^4 {{a_k}{e^{{{jk2\pi m} \over 5}}}} $$, where $${a_0} = 1,{a_1} = 3j,{a_2} = 2j,{a_3} = - 2j$$ and $${a_4} = - 3j$$. The value of the sum $$\sum\limits_{n = 0}^4 {x[n]\sin {{4\pi n} \over 5}} $$ is
explanation:
Given : $x[n]=\sum\limits_{k=0}^4 a_k e^{j \frac{k 2 \pi n}{5}}$

where, $a_0=1, a_1=3 j, a_2=2 j, a_3=-2 j$ and $a_4=-3 j$

$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \sum_{n=0}^4 x(n) \sin \frac{4 \pi n}{5}=\sum_{n=0}^4 x(n)\left[\frac{e^{\frac{j 4 \pi n}{5}}-e^{\frac{-j 4 \pi n}{5}}}{2 j}\right] \\\\ & \sum_{n=0}^4 x(n) \sin \frac{4 \pi n}{5}=\frac{1}{2 j}\left[\sum_{n=0}^4 x(n) e^{\frac{j 2 \pi(2) n}{5}}-\sum_{n=0}^4 x(n) e^{\frac{-j 2 \pi(2) n}{5}}\right] .......(i)\end{aligned}$

DTFS coefficient is given by,

$$ a_k=\frac{1}{N} \sum\limits_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{\frac{-j 2 \pi k n}{N}} $$

Given that time period of $x(n)$ is $N=5 \mathrm{sec}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & a_k=\frac{1}{5} \sum\limits_{n=0}^4 x(n) e^{\frac{-j 2 \pi k n}{5}} \\ & \therefore \quad \sum_{n=0}^4 x(n) e^{\frac{-j 2 \pi k n}{5}}=5 a_k \end{aligned} $$

If $k=-2$ then $\sum\limits_{n=0}^4 x(n) e^{\frac{j 2 \pi(2) n}{5}}=5 a_{-2}$ ........(ii)

If $k=2$ then $\sum\limits_{n=0}^4 x(n) e^{\frac{-j 2 \pi(2) n}{5}}=5 a_2$ ........(iii)

Substituting equation (ii) and (iii) in equation (i), we get

$$ \sum\limits_{n=0}^4 x(n) \sin \frac{4 \pi n}{5}=\frac{1}{2 j}\left[5 a_{-2}-5 a_2\right] $$ ........(iv)

From the property of discrete Fourier series.

$a_k=a_{k+N}$, where $N$ is the time period.

$$ \therefore a_{-2}=a_{-2+5}=a_3 $$

So, equation (iv) is becomes,

$$ \sum\limits_{n=0}^4 x(n) \sin \frac{4 \pi n}{5}=\frac{1}{2 j}\left[5 a_3-5 a_2\right]=\frac{1}{2 j}[5 \times(-2 j)-5 \times 2 j]=-10 $$

Hence, the correct option is $(\mathrm{A})$.

content:
Consider the signals $x[n]=2^{n-1} u[-n+2]$ and $y[n]=2^{-n+2} u[n+1]$, where $u[n]$ is the unit step sequence. Let $X\left(e^{j \omega}\right)$ and $Y\left(e^{j \omega}\right)$ be the discrete-time Fourier transform of $x[n]$ and $y[n]$, respectively. The value of the integral

$$ \frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_o^{2 \pi} X\left(e^{j \omega}\right) Y\left(e^{-j \omega}\right) d \omega $$

(round off to one decimal place) is $\_\_\_\_$
explanation:
Solution: As mentioned, we have, $x[n]=2^{n-1} u[-n+2]$

Also,   $$ y[n]=2^{-n+2} u[n+1] $$

Now on proceeding further we have

$$ y[n] \stackrel{\text { DTFT }}{\longleftrightarrow} Y\left(e^{j w}\right) $$

or,  $$ y[-n] \stackrel{\text { DTFT }}{\longleftrightarrow} Y\left(e^{-j w}\right)

then,  $$ \therefore \quad y[-n]=2^{n+2} u[-n+1] $$

On putting the values, we get

$$ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_0^{2 \pi} x\left(e^{j w}\right) y\left(e^{-j w}\right) d w=\left[\left.x[n] * y[-n]\right|_{n=0}\right. $$

Hence, $x[n]^* y[-n]=\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} 2^{k-1} u[-k+2] 2^{n-k+2} u[k-n+1]$

or,  $$ =\sum_{k=-\infty}^2 2^{n+1} u[k-n+1] $$

Now solving for,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left.x[n] * y[-n]\right|_{n=0}=\sum_{k=-\infty}^2 2 u[k+1] \\ & =\sum_{k=-1}^2 2=2+2+2+2 \\ & =8 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is (8 to 8).
